topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the locker room was deafening, punctuated only by the sharp, metallic click of a locker door closing. Liam stared at the acceptance letter, the glossy paper feeling strangely heavy in his trembling hands. He’d poured every ounce of his being into this season, the early mornings, the grueling physical therapy, the sacrifices his family had made. Now, the official roster listed his name only in the supplementary notes, a footnote rather than a headline. He slowly folded the paper, the creases sharp under his thumb. A tightness began to coil in his chest, a cold, hard knot that made drawing a full breath feel like an effort. He looked up, meeting the eyes of his teammate, Marcus, who offered a small, sympathetic nod. Liam managed a tight, humorless smile, forcing his shoulders to relax, though the muscles beneath his jersey felt suddenly rigid and uncooperative.

[story 2]

"So, that's it," Sarah whispered, the words barely audible over the hum of the ventilation system. She traced the outline of the team roster with a finger, her gaze fixed on the empty space where her name should have been. I felt a strange hollowness expand in my stomach, a sudden lightness followed by an immediate, crushing weight. I picked up my water bottle, the plastic cold against my palm, and took a long, slow sip, trying to anchor myself. Every movement felt clumsy, exaggerated. When Coach finally walked in, his expression unreadable, I stood up too quickly, nearly knocking over my chair. My voice, when I finally spoke, came out higher pitched than intended, a thin, reedy sound that made me immediately want to disappear.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the administrative office seemed to burn brighter than usual, reflecting off the polished mahogany desk. I reread the email three times, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. My hands began to shake uncontrollably; I had to press them flat against the cool surface of the desk to steady them. It wasn't just the disappointment; it was the sudden, dizzying disorientation. Everything felt slightly skewed, as if the floor had tilted beneath my feet. I cleared my throat, trying to summon the practiced confidence I usually wore like armor, but the effort felt monumental. "I... I understand," I managed, the words catching in my throat before dissolving into a ragged exhale.
